title: Prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of the overtraining syndrome: joint consensus statement of the European College of Sport Science and the American College of Sports Medicine
authors: ["Romain Meeusen","Martine Duclos","Carl Foster","Andrew Fry","Michael Gleeson","David Nieman","John Raglin","Gerard Rietjens","Juergen Steinacker","Axel Urhausen"]
population: Athletes exposed to sustained heavy training loads
finding: Persistent fatigue, performance decrement, sleep disturbance, mood change, and illness/injury signs require conservative management and exclusion of medical causes.
practical_application: Treat red-flag symptom clusters as a reason to stop performance-seeking recommendations and bias toward recovery or medical evaluation.
limits: Consensus guidance for overtraining syndrome, not a diagnostic engine for acute injury or illness.
notes: Supports fail-closed behavior when athlete state is unsafe.

title: International Olympic Committee consensus statement on load in sport and risk of injury: how much is too much?
authors: ["Torbjoern Soligard","Martin Schwellnus","Juan-M. Alonso","Roald Bahr","Ben Clarsen","H. Paul Dijkstra","Tim Gabbett","Michael Gleeson","Martin Hagglund","Mark R. Hutchinson","Christa Janse van Rensburg","Karim M. Khan","Romain Meeusen","John W. Orchard","Babette M. Pluim","Martin Raftery","Richard Budgett","Lars Engebretsen"]
finding: Injury risk is related to both external and internal load, with rapid load changes and inadequate recovery increasing risk.
practical_application: Reduce or defer load after unusually hard efforts, high perceived exertion, or stacked stressors instead of adding quality work.
limits: Broad multi-sport consensus; individual thresholds require athlete-specific history.
notes: Primary source for conservative load adjustment.

title: Continued sports activity, using a pain-monitoring model, during rehabilitation in patients with Achilles tendinopathy: a randomized controlled study
authors: ["Karin Graevare Silbernagel","Roland Thomee","Bengt I. Eriksson","Jon Karlsson"]
finding: Achilles loading activity during rehab did not show negative effects when governed by a pain-monitoring model.
practical_application: Use Achilles symptom severity to gate pacing and quality workouts; controlled loading may continue only inside conservative pain limits.
limits: Clinical tendinopathy population; does not authorize ignoring worsening pain, red flags, or professional evaluation.
notes: Supports symptom-gated Achilles decisions rather than blanket rest or blanket continuation.

title: Sleep Interventions Designed to Improve Athletic Performance and Recovery: A Systematic Review of Current Approaches
population: Athletes studied in sleep intervention research
finding: Sleep is important for aspects of athletic performance and recovery, although intervention evidence varies by population and protocol.
practical_application: Treat poor sleep as a load-management input and reduce the next workout when paired with fatigue or hard prior efforts.
limits: Systematic review notes heterogeneous methods and incomplete guidance for all athlete groups.
notes: Supports conservative handling of poor sleep.

title: Training stress, neuromuscular fatigue and well-being in volleyball: a systematic review
authors: ["André Rebelo","João R. Pereira","Paulo Cunha","Manuel J. Coelho-e-Silva","João Valente-dos-Santos"]
journal_or_publisher: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
population: Volleyball athletes across youth, collegiate, professional, male, and female cohorts in longitudinal monitoring studies
finding: Volleyball monitoring literature links training stress measures with neuromuscular fatigue and well-being assessments, including daily internal load and jump-based external load monitoring.
practical_application: Treat volleyball or jumping exposure as a neuromuscular load signal to consider alongside athlete-reported fatigue, soreness, and readiness; it does not quantify next-day running risk by itself.
limits: Systematic review of volleyball monitoring studies; does not quantify next-day running risk or prescribe running-specific recovery thresholds.
notes: Retains Task 5 provisional rule identity with auditable source backing.

title: American College of Sports Medicine position stand. Quantity and quality of exercise for developing and maintaining cardiorespiratory, musculoskeletal, and neuromotor fitness in apparently healthy adults
authors: ["Carol Ewing Garber","Bryan Blissmer","Michael R. Deschenes","Barry A. Franklin","Michael J. Lamonte","I-Min Lee","David C. Nieman","David P. Swain"]
finding: Regular cardiorespiratory and resistance exercise supports maintenance and improvement of physical fitness.
practical_application: When guardrails are clear, maintain the planned easy or long aerobic work rather than adding unnecessary complexity.
limits: General adult prescription guidance, not runner-specific periodization.
notes: General consistency source for default maintain decisions.

title: Quantifying training intensity distribution in elite endurance athletes: is there evidence for an optimal distribution?
journal_or_publisher: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
finding: Observed endurance training was organized with most sessions below the first ventilatory threshold and a smaller share of high-intensity work.
practical_application: Keep most weekly running easy and avoid turning easy or long runs into threshold work.
limits: Descriptive evidence from elite skiers; not a universal prescription for recreational runners.
notes: Used as intensity-distribution evidence, not as rigid 80/20 dogma.

title: Effect of training on lactate/ventilatory thresholds: a meta-analysis
population: Sedentary and active subjects in threshold-training studies
finding: Training near lactate or ventilatory threshold can improve thresholds, with conditioned athletes often needing higher intensities.
practical_application: Use threshold work as a bounded quality stimulus, and defer it when recovery or injury guardrails are active.
limits: Meta-analysis spans mixed populations and protocols; pace prescriptions still need individual calibration.
notes: Supports threshold as a valid but bounded training stimulus.

title: Effects of Strength Training on Running Economy in Highly Trained Runners: A Systematic Review With Meta-Analysis of Controlled Trials
journal_or_publisher: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
population: Highly trained middle- and long-distance runners
finding: Strength training programs can improve running economy in trained runners.
practical_application: Include strength as supportive training, while avoiding heavy strength work when recovery flags suggest load reduction.
limits: Focused on trained runners and controlled trials; transfer depends on exercise selection and recovery management.
notes: Uses the DOI-backed 2016 Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research meta-analysis.

title: Athletic Records and Human Endurance
finding: Race time scales with distance by t2 = t1 * (d2/d1)^1.06, enabling cross-distance performance prediction.
practical_application: Project half-marathon pace and training zones from a recent shorter race; a conservative exponent (1.08) suits recreational runners.
limits: An empirical model; individual endurance profiles and training specificity shift the true exponent.
notes: Backs the Riegel projection used for pace zones and goal feasibility.
